The SOD1 protein is a crucial component of the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC) and plays a crucial role in the oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) pathway.
The SOD1 protein is a crucial component of the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC) and plays a crucial role in the oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) pathway.
Mutations in the SOD1 gene are the most common cause of familial ALS (fALS) and sporadic ALS (sALS).
The SOD1 gene encodes a superoxide dismutase (SOD) protein that catalyzes the conversion of superoxide anions (O2•−) into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and oxygen.

The C9orf72 gene encodes a hexanucleotide repeat expansion of the C9orf72 gene.
The repeat unit is located in the 5′ untranslated region (UTR) of the C9orf72 gene.
VCP mutations are found in approximately 1% of ALS cases, and the majority of these mutations are located in the C-terminal domain of the VCP protein.
VCP mutations are associated with a wide range of clinical phenotypes, including motor neuron disease (MND), progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP), and spinal muscular atrophy (SMA).

This expansion results in the formation of a toxic protein, TDP-43, which aggregates in the cytoplasm of neurons and glial cells, leading to the formation of Lewy bodies (LBs) and the formation of neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs).

This expansion results in the formation of a toxic protein, TDP-43, which aggregates in the cytoplasm of neurons and glial cells, leading to the formation of Lewy bodies (LBs) and the formation of neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs).
The most common genetic cause of ALS is a C9orf72 repeat expansion in the first exon of the gene encoding TAR DNA-binding protein 43 (TDP-43).

The SOD1 protein is a crucial component of the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC) and plays a crucial role in the oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) pathway.
The SOD1 protein is a crucial component of the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC) and plays a crucial role in the oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) pathway.
Mutations in the SOD1 gene are the most common cause of familial ALS (fALS) and sporadic ALS (sALS).
The SOD1 gene encodes a superoxide dismutase (SOD) protein that catalyzes the conversion of superoxide anions (O2•−) into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and oxygen.

The C9orf72 gene encodes a hexanucleotide repeat expansion of the C9orf72 gene.
The repeat unit is located in the 5′ untranslated region (UTR) of the C9orf72 gene.
VCP mutations are found in approximately 1% of ALS cases, and the majority of these mutations are located in the C-terminal domain of the VCP protein.
VCP mutations are associated with a wide range of clinical phenotypes, including motor neuron disease (MND), progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP), and spinal muscular atrophy (SMA).

This expansion results in the formation of a toxic protein, TDP-43, which aggregates in the cytoplasm of neurons and glial cells, leading to the formation of Lewy bodies (LBs) and the formation of neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs).

This expansion results in the formation of a toxic protein, TDP-43, which aggregates in the cytoplasm of neurons and glial cells, leading to the formation of Lewy bodies (LBs) and the formation of neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs).
The most common genetic cause of ALS is a C9orf72 repeat expansion in the first exon of the gene encoding TAR DNA-binding protein 43 (TDP-43).
